title: Living in Boulder vlog 5 - 8 - 2018: Google Earthing Boulder historic photos

enrichment.summary: Kyle gives a late-night update on a long-term project where he geolocates historic Boulder, Colorado photos (some ~100 years old) and maps them in a 3D/360 environment. He describes the tedious process of matching old photos to modern locations, dealing with missing buildings, and using Denver Post geo-tagged archives to help place oil derrick photos from around 1902.
enrichment.speaker: kyle

stances.position: Finds it deeply worthwhile despite being a time-consuming 'black hole'
Igniting interest in history is one of the most important things possible, equating it to interest in science
Plans to merge Boulder photos, mountain photos, and Boulder realm into one unified project

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I've been working on a project involving taking photos of Boulder from a hundred years ago and finding where they were taken.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I'm creating 360-degree views from these old photos if they're near a road or if I can get an actual 360.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I plan to use these 360 views in a cartoon world, including a line that shows the direction and how far you can see in the photo.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: There are 900 photos of Boulder within the town, plus more I haven't counted yet.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I uploaded the photos to Flickr but haven't set them all to public yet because it's a tedious process.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I was stuck on photos from the year 1900 for a long time because many were taken by Joseph Sturgan.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I'm currently working on photos from around 1902, particularly those involving oil derricks.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: It's challenging to pinpoint where some of these old photos were taken because the landscape has changed so much.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I found that the Denver Post has locations for some of these photos, which helped me place about 110 of them.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Some photos show houses that aren't on current maps, so I might have to build virtual houses to match the old photos.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: When I successfully line up an old photo with its location, it feels like a victory.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: My goal is to find a plausible location for each photo even if I can't be certain of the exact spot.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I'm also planning to work on mountain photos and rebuild the mountains in Boulder as they appeared in the past.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I'm trying to combine this project with two others I'm working on, bouncing between the three to stay productive.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: This project is time-consuming but incredibly interesting because it feels like teleporting back in time.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: It's fascinating to think about the generations of people who lived in a completely different Boulder and had great lives.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I believe one of the most important things I can do is ignite interest in history, just like igniting interest in science.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Science and history are interconnected, with science being a form of deep history, like the geologic time scale.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: I hope to finish working on the Boulder photos soon and move on to the mountain photos next.

transcript.text: Hello, Living in Boulder V-Log. It's 1225 in the morning, and it's been a few days since I did my last V-Log, so I didn't do a V-Log because I was working on the same project, stuck in deep space.
I'm trying to find, I'm taking all the photos of Boulder from 100 years ago, and I'm finding where they were taken, and then I'm doing a 360.
If I can get the photo near a road where I can get an actual 360, I use that, and so then I'll have to come through again and then use the 360 in Cartoon World.

I'll need two of them, but then I have the line that shows which direction you're going, you're looking in the photo, and how far you can see what you're looking at, and there were 900 photos of Boulder within the town, and then another, I don't know, I haven't counted yet, but I uploaded them to Flickr.
I didn't have all of that set to public. I tried to set my folder of Boulder to public, but it didn't let me. I'm going to have to do them all individually, and I haven't gotten around to that.
It's like 900 photos I have to do, so it'll have to take a while.
So today I was stuck. I was stuck on 1900 for a long time, because that's the year that a lot of people, that most of the photos from Joseph Sturgen, people guessed that you took them.
So I finally got through there, and I think I'm in like the year 2005, or 1905.
And I'm working on the oil derricks right now, and that's really hard, because I don't know exactly where these photos were taken.
And I, before, when I built this a couple of years ago, I guessed where they were.
I think I just threw a bunch down, just from the photos.
But then, I did a search yesterday, and I'm sure I did a search before, but yesterday I did a search, and I found on the Denver Post, they actually have the locations on them.
So I put a bunch of locations, a bunch of the ones down where they know the name of them and the location, for over 100, like 110 or something, in the Boulder area.
There's like 1,000 or more, if you're going to include the front range.
It looks like Longmont was the most densely populated, and it looked like they had 1,000 up there.
So, and some of them are still in use.
I didn't know that.
So, yeah, I was looking at one photo.
Well, yeah, I built those, so that's what I had to do.
That took a while.

And then I was looking at a photo today for like an hour or something or two, just trying to figure out what the hell I was going to do with it, where I was going to put it, because it didn't make sense.
It didn't line up.
I couldn't find, you know, the photo.
And that's the same thing with the photos in Boulder.
When I go to a place where I think the photo's taken, there's houses in the photo that are not in the map.
So, and that happens over and over again.
And so I think I'm going to have to even like build a bunch of houses to accommodate the photos.
So, I love it when I line it up.
It's like score.
So, yeah, so I was working on the Derricks one now.
You know, there's some that I don't know yet.
So, you know, try to find something.
My goal is to, if I don't know where it is taken from, at least I, my goal is to find a place where I don't have any reason to think that it's not there.
You know, so that I can just assume it was, you know.
So, yeah.
It says 1902 loading trains.
And I think I skipped a few.
So, that's where I am, 1902.
And then I'm going to go into the mountains, the mountain photos.
And rebuild the mountains in Boulder.

And until I crack, I hope that, I think, I'm going to try to make it so that Google Earth in Boulder, Google Earth in Rome, and Google Earth in, hmm, Boulder Rome, which I actually have a, I'm pretty far along.
I didn't realize.
Try to make those three the one project that I just kind of bounce around between the three.
Try to stay away from Rome, though.
That's a black hole.
But, but this is turning into a bit of a time gobbler.
But, you know, it's still super interesting, just teleporting back in time in Boulder and familiarizing myself with the whole world.
There's entire generations of people who lived here who lived in a completely different Boulder and had great lives and did all sorts of fun stuff.
And it looked totally different, but it was Boulder, and it was just, you know, our grandparents.
So, it's interesting.
We need to bring it back.
So, yeah, that's one of the most important things I think we can do in the world is ignite our interest in history, because that's the same thing as igniting our interest in science.
Because science is history, science is just deep history, geologic time scale.
So, it's all related.
So, anyways, I wrote, I wore our shirt today.
6.10, okay.
Later.
Hopefully, next time I do a vlog, maybe if it's even in the whole week.
That would be awesome, maybe if I'm at least done with the photos of Boulder.
And I'm working on the mountains of Boulder, okay.
